Bluetooth

slides.title: Bluetooth is a wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances
lines: Bluetooth is a wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances
First proposed by telecom vendor Ericsson in 1994
Originally conceived as a wireless alternative to RS-232 data cables
slides.text:
Bluetooth is a wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances
ISM band from 2.4 to 2.485 GHz
fixed and mobile devices
building personal area networks(PANs)
First proposed by telecom vendor Ericsson in 1994
Originally conceived as a wireless alternative to RS-232 data cables
What is Bluetooth?

slides.title: Bluetooth is managed by the
lines: founded in 1998  by a group of companies
more than 20,000 member companies as of 2014
, but the standard is no longer maintained.
The SIG oversees the development of the specification, manages the qualification program, and protects the trademarks.
slides.text:
Bluetooth is managed by the
Bluetooth Special Interest Group
(SIG),
founded in 1998  by a group of companies
more than 20,000 member companies as of 2014
Bluetooth was standardized as
IEEE 802.15.1
, but the standard is no longer maintained.
The SIG oversees the development of the specification, manages the qualification program, and protects the trademarks.
Bluetooth Special Interest Group

slides.title: The 10th Century king of Denmark Harald "Bluetooth"
lines: The 10th Century king of Denmark Harald "Bluetooth"
united all the tiny island around Denmark, southern Sweden, and southern Norway into a single country.
He got his name from his penchant for blueberries.
Bluetooth, the new low-cost radio technology, is designed to unite or connect all different types of devices to effectively work as one.
slides.text:
The 10th Century king of Denmark Harald "Bluetooth"
Gormsson
united all the tiny island around Denmark, southern Sweden, and southern Norway into a single country.
He got his name from his penchant for blueberries.
Bluetooth, the new low-cost radio technology, is designed to unite or connect all different types of devices to effectively work as one.
The Name: “Bluetooth”

slides.title: Collection of devices connected in an ad hoc fashion
lines: Collection of devices connected in an ad hoc fashion
One unit will act as master
Sets clock and frequency hopping pattern
Can connect to 7 active or 255 inactive (parked) slaves
Determines bit rate allocated to each slave
All devices synchronized to a common clock and hopping sequence.
Slaves can communicate only with the master

Sets clock and frequency hopping pattern

Unique frequency hopping pattern/ID
All devices synchronized to a common clock and hopping sequence.
Slaves can communicate only with the master

slides.title: When the
lines: is established, the Master Clock (CLK) is communicated to the slaves.
, which is determined by fields in the device address and the clock of the master.
The basic hopping pattern is a pseudo-random ordering of the 79 frequencies in the ISM band.

Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) is used to improve the performance of physical links in the presence of interference as well as reducing the interference caused by physical links on other devices in the ISM band.
AFH uses less than the full 79 frequencies that the basic
slides.text:
When the
piconet
is established, the Master Clock (CLK) is communicated to the slaves.
Devices in a
piconet
use a specific
frequency hopping pattern
, which is determined by fields in the device address and the clock of the master.
The basic hopping pattern is a pseudo-random ordering of the 79 frequencies in the ISM band.
Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) is used to improve the performance of physical links in the presence of interference as well as reducing the interference caused by physical links on other devices in the ISM band.
AFH uses less than the full 79 frequencies that the basic
piconet
uses.
Physical Layer: Frequency Hopping

slides.title: The basic
lines: physical channel is divided into time slots, each 625
A time division duplex (TDD) scheme is used where master and slave alternatively transmit.
The packet start should be aligned with the slot start. Packets may extend over up to five time slots.
slides.text:
The basic
piconet
physical channel is divided into time slots, each 625
μs
in length.
A time division duplex (TDD) scheme is used where master and slave alternatively transmit.
The packet start should be aligned with the slot start. Packets may extend over up to five time slots.
Physical Layer:
Time Slots

slides.title: The Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) is taking care of current and future versions of this industry standard
lines: The Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) is taking care of current and future versions of this industry standard
For Bluetooth v1.1 and Bluetooth v1.2 there has been a collaboration with IEEE802.15.1
Afterwards the IEEE Study Group 802.15.1b voted unanimously to discontinue the relation to Bluetooth SIG
later versions of Bluetooth did not become IEEE standards
Bluetooth v2.0 with optional Enhanced Data Rate (EDR) has been published in November 2004.
combination of GFSK and DPSK to increase data rate to 3Mbps
Secure simple pairing protocol speeds up pairing procedure
Bluetooth v3.0 + HS (high speed) published April 2009
used for high data rate (up to 24Mbps)
UWB Multiband was candidate PHY but was dropped due to IPR problems
Bluetooth PHY used for control channels and medium rate traffic
Bluetooth Smart (v4.0) published June 2010: Classic Bluetooth,
low energy protocol originates from the
LE not compatible with other Bluetooth modes
2x the data rate, 4x the range, 8x the broadcast capability
slides.text:
The Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) is taking care of current and future versions of this industry standard
For Bluetooth v1.1 and Bluetooth v1.2 there has been a collaboration with IEEE802.15.1
IEEE Standards 802.15.1-2002 and 802.15.1-2005
Afterwards the IEEE Study Group 802.15.1b voted unanimously to discontinue the relation to Bluetooth SIG
later versions of Bluetooth did not become IEEE standards
Bluetooth v2.0 with optional Enhanced Data Rate (EDR) has been published in November 2004.
combination of GFSK and DPSK to increase data rate to 3Mbps
Recent History of Bluetooth
Bluetooth v2.1 + EDR (July 2007)
Secure simple pairing protocol speeds up pairing procedure
Bluetooth v3.0 + HS (high speed) published April 2009
alternate PHY based on
WiFi
used for high data rate (up to 24Mbps)
UWB Multiband was candidate PHY but was dropped due to IPR problems
Bluetooth PHY used for control channels and medium rate traffic
Bluetooth Smart (v4.0) published June 2010: Classic Bluetooth,
Bluetooth+HS
and Bluetooth Low Energy
low energy protocol originates from the
Wibree
system (Nokia);
LE not compatible with other Bluetooth modes
Bluetooth v5 (June 2016)
2x the data rate, 4x the range, 8x the broadcast capability

slides.title: New low power design, no downward compatibility to Classic Bluetooth (CB)
lines: New low power design, no downward compatibility to Classic Bluetooth (CB)
To  be used in sensor networks
sensors transmit short data packets with a low duty cycle
Reuse to as much as possible Classic Bluetooth hardware
Simplified physical layer as compared to Classic Bluetooth
three advertising channels (CB uses 16 or 32 for Inquiry and Page) used for connection process
slides.text:
New low power design, no downward compatibility to Classic Bluetooth (CB)
To  be used in sensor networks
sensors transmit short data packets with a low duty cycle
support low latency
not optimized for streaming data
asymmetric node complexity (slave - master)
Reuse to as much as possible Classic Bluetooth hardware
Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE, Smart Bluetooth)
Simplified physical layer as compared to Classic Bluetooth
three advertising channels (CB uses 16 or 32 for Inquiry and Page) used for connection process
37x2MHz data channels (CB: 79x1MHz)
increased modulation index of GMSK
3ms latency from
nonconnected
state (CB: >100ms)

slides.title: Authentication
lines: Verifies the identification of the devices that are communicating in the channel.
Protecting the data from the attacker by allowing only authorized users to access the data.
Only authorized users have control over the resources.
slides.text:
Authentication
Verifies the identification of the devices that are communicating in the channel.
Confidentiality
Protecting the data from the attacker by allowing only authorized users to access the data.
Authorization
Only authorized users have control over the resources.
Bluetooth Security

slides.title: Security Mode 1
slides.text:
Security Mode 1
Non-Secure Mode
Security Mode 2
Service level enforced security mode
Security Mode 3
Link-level enforced security mode
Security
Modes
of Bluetooth

slides.title: Only security at this level is by the nature of the connection: data-hopping and short-distance
lines: Only security at this level is by the nature of the connection: data-hopping and short-distance
Bluetooth devices transmit over the unlicensed 2.45GHz radio band, the same band used by microwave ovens and cordless phones.(FHSS)
All Bluetooth devices employ “data-hopping”, which entails skipping around the radio band up to 1600 times per second, at 1MHz intervals (79 different frequencies)
Most connections are less than 10 meters, so there is a limit as to eavesdropping possibilities
slides.text:
Only security at this level is by the nature of the connection: data-hopping and short-distance
Bluetooth devices transmit over the unlicensed 2.45GHz radio band, the same band used by microwave ovens and cordless phones.(FHSS)
All Bluetooth devices employ “data-hopping”, which entails skipping around the radio band up to 1600 times per second, at 1MHz intervals (79 different frequencies)
Most connections are less than 10 meters, so there is a limit as to eavesdropping possibilities
Mode 1: No Security

slides.title: Service Access depends on device:
lines: Trusted devices have unrestricted access to all services, fixed relationship to other devices
Untrusted devices generally have no permanent relationship and services that it has access to are limited.
Unfortunately, all services on a device are given the same security policy, other than application layer add-ons.
slides.text:
Service Access depends on device:
Trusted devices have unrestricted access to all services, fixed relationship to other devices
Untrusted devices generally have no permanent relationship and services that it has access to are limited.
Unfortunately, all services on a device are given the same security policy, other than application layer add-ons.
Mode 2: Service Level Security

slides.title: Services can have one of 3 security levels:
lines: Services can have one of 3 security levels:
: Requires Authentication and Authorization.  PIN number must be entered.
: Open to all devices, the default level. Security for legacy applications, for example.
slides.text:
Services can have one of 3 security levels:
Level 3
: Requires Authentication and Authorization.  PIN number must be entered.
Level 2
: Authentication only, fixed PIN ok.
Level 1
: Open to all devices, the default level. Security for legacy applications, for example.
Mode 2: Service Security Levels

slides.title: Security is implemented by symmetric keys in a challenge-response system.
lines: Security is implemented by symmetric keys in a challenge-response system.
Security implementations in Bluetooth units are all the same, and are publicly available:
PIN, BD_ADDR, RAND(), Link and Encryption Keys
slides.text:
Security is implemented by symmetric keys in a challenge-response system.
Security implementations in Bluetooth units are all the same, and are publicly available:
http://www.bluetooth.com/pdf/Bluetooth_11_Specifications_Book.pdf
Critical ingredients
PIN, BD_ADDR, RAND(), Link and Encryption Keys
Mode 3: Link level security

slides.title: 0x2
lines: 受信任：设备已与另一设备建立固定关系，且对所有服务的访问不受限制。
不受信任：虽然已成功通过身份验证，但设备只能访问一组受限制的服务。
slides.text:
0x2
蓝牙的信任模式
受信任：设备已与另一设备建立固定关系，且对所有服务的访问不受限制。
不受信任：虽然已成功通过身份验证，但设备只能访问一组受限制的服务。

slides.title: Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security
lines: Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security
Strength of the Random Number Generator (RNG) is unknown.
RNG may produce periodic numbers that reduces the strength of authentication mechanism.
Such weak PINs are used to generate link and encryption keys that are easily predictable.
More robust initialization key generation procedure should be developed.
As only device authentication is provided, application security and user authentication can be employed.
Stream cipher is weak and key length is negotiable.
Robust encryption procedure and minimum key length should be decided and passed as an agreement.
slides.text:
Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security
Security Issue
Remarks
Strength of the Random Number Generator (RNG) is unknown.
RNG may produce periodic numbers that reduces the strength of authentication mechanism.
Short PINs are allowed.
Such weak PINs are used to generate link and encryption keys that are easily predictable.
Encryption key length is negotiable.
More robust initialization key generation procedure should be developed.
No user authentication exists.
As only device authentication is provided, application security and user authentication can be employed.
Stream cipher is weak and key length is negotiable.
Robust encryption procedure and minimum key length should be decided and passed as an agreement.

slides.title: Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security (Cont’d)
lines: Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security (Cont’d)
Privacy can be compromised if the BD_ADDR is captured and associated with a particular user.
Once the BD_ADDR is associated with a particular user, that user’s activity can be logged. So, loss of privacy can be compromised.
Device authentication is simple shared key challenge response.
One-way authentication may be subjected to man-in-middle attacks. Mutual authentication is a good idea to provide verification.
slides.text:
Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security (Cont’d)
Security Issue
Remarks
Privacy can be compromised if the BD_ADDR is captured and associated with a particular user.
Once the BD_ADDR is associated with a particular user, that user’s activity can be logged. So, loss of privacy can be compromised.
Device authentication is simple shared key challenge response.
One-way authentication may be subjected to man-in-middle attacks. Mutual authentication is a good idea to provide verification.

Causes harm when the user sends the data to the other user.

full_text:
Bluetooth Security and Privacy
Instructor: Jinsong
Han
Wireless network and security

In the 2000s, the
age of the smartphone
, this figure rose to 2 billion.
By 2021 there will be 48 billion devices connected to the internet, in what we’re likely to term
the age of the IoT
.
Bluetooth

Bluetooth is a wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances
ISM band from 2.4 to 2.485 GHz
fixed and mobile devices
building personal area networks(PANs)
First proposed by telecom vendor Ericsson in 1994
Originally conceived as a wireless alternative to RS-232 data cables
What is Bluetooth?

Bluetooth is managed by the
Bluetooth Special Interest Group
(SIG),
founded in 1998  by a group of companies
more than 20,000 member companies as of 2014
Bluetooth was standardized as
IEEE 802.15.1
, but the standard is no longer maintained.
The SIG oversees the development of the specification, manages the qualification program, and protects the trademarks.
Bluetooth Special Interest Group

The 10th Century king of Denmark Harald "Bluetooth"
Gormsson
united all the tiny island around Denmark, southern Sweden, and southern Norway into a single country.
He got his name from his penchant for blueberries.
Bluetooth, the new low-cost radio technology, is designed to unite or connect all different types of devices to effectively work as one.
The Name: “Bluetooth”

Audio and data peripherals
Headsets, Speakers, …
Keyboards, Cameras, Printers, …
Intelligent devices
Cellular phones, laptops, …
Embedded applications
Cars, Industrial systems, …
Bluetooth Applications

Unlicensed 2.4 GHz radio band: ISM (industrial, scientific, medical) band – available worldwide
Gross data rate of 1 Mbit/s (EDR: 3Mbit/s, HS: 24Mbit/s)
Basic 10m range extended to 100m with amplifiers
TDMA-TDD-Slow Frequency Hopping spread spectrum
Supports up to 8 devices in a
piconet
(1 master and 7 slaves)
Piconets
can combine to form
scatternets
Mixed voice/data connections possible
Encryption
Ubiquitous radio link
Bluetooth Characteristics

OSI and Bluetooth Protocol

Collection of devices connected in an ad hoc fashion
One unit will act as master
Sets clock and frequency hopping pattern
Can connect to 7 active or 255 inactive (parked) slaves
Determines bit rate allocated to each slave
Unique frequency hopping pattern/ID
All devices synchronized to a common clock and hopping sequence.
Slaves can communicate only with the master
Communication Topology: Bluetooth
Piconet
Master
Slave 1
Unit C
Unit D
Unit B
Slave 2
Slave 3
Unit A
Unit E

A device may participate concurrently in two or more
piconets
on time-division multiplexing (TDM) basis.
A device can be both master and slave.
A device can never be a master of more than one
piconet
.
Two or more
piconets
that include one or more devices participating in more than one
piconet
form a
scatternet
.
Communication Topology: Bluetooth
Scatternet
Master A
Slave A1
Common
slave
Slave A2
Master B
Slave B1
Slave B2
Slave B3

When the
piconet
is established, the Master Clock (CLK) is communicated to the slaves.
Devices in a
piconet
use a specific
frequency hopping pattern
, which is determined by fields in the device address and the clock of the master.
The basic hopping pattern is a pseudo-random ordering of the 79 frequencies in the ISM band.
Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) is used to improve the performance of physical links in the presence of interference as well as reducing the interference caused by physical links on other devices in the ISM band.
AFH uses less than the full 79 frequencies that the basic
piconet
uses.
Physical Layer: Frequency Hopping

The basic
piconet
physical channel is divided into time slots, each 625
μs
in length.
A time division duplex (TDD) scheme is used where master and slave alternatively transmit.
The packet start should be aligned with the slot start. Packets may extend over up to five time slots.
Physical Layer:
Time Slots

The Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) is taking care of current and future versions of this industry standard
For Bluetooth v1.1 and Bluetooth v1.2 there has been a collaboration with IEEE802.15.1
IEEE Standards 802.15.1-2002 and 802.15.1-2005
Afterwards the IEEE Study Group 802.15.1b voted unanimously to discontinue the relation to Bluetooth SIG
later versions of Bluetooth did not become IEEE standards
Bluetooth v2.0 with optional Enhanced Data Rate (EDR) has been published in November 2004.
combination of GFSK and DPSK to increase data rate to 3Mbps
Recent History of Bluetooth
Bluetooth v2.1 + EDR (July 2007)
Secure simple pairing protocol speeds up pairing procedure
Bluetooth v3.0 + HS (high speed) published April 2009
alternate PHY based on
WiFi
used for high data rate (up to 24Mbps)
UWB Multiband was candidate PHY but was dropped due to IPR problems
Bluetooth PHY used for control channels and medium rate traffic
Bluetooth Smart (v4.0) published June 2010: Classic Bluetooth,
Bluetooth+HS
and Bluetooth Low Energy
low energy protocol originates from the
Wibree
system (Nokia);
LE not compatible with other Bluetooth modes
Bluetooth v5 (June 2016)
2x the data rate, 4x the range, 8x the broadcast capability

New low power design, no downward compatibility to Classic Bluetooth (CB)
To  be used in sensor networks
sensors transmit short data packets with a low duty cycle
support low latency
not optimized for streaming data
asymmetric node complexity (slave - master)
Reuse to as much as possible Classic Bluetooth hardware
Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE, Smart Bluetooth)
Simplified physical layer as compared to Classic Bluetooth
three advertising channels (CB uses 16 or 32 for Inquiry and Page) used for connection process
37x2MHz data channels (CB: 79x1MHz)
increased modulation index of GMSK
3ms latency from
nonconnected
state (CB: >100ms)

Dual Mode Chipsets (Bluetooth Smart Ready)

Classic Bluetooth Versus Bluetooth Smart
Technical Specification
Classic Bluetooth technology
Bluetooth Smart technology
Distance/Range (theoretical max.)
100 m (330 ft)
<100 m (<330 ft)
Over the air data rate
1–3 Mbit/s
1 Mbit/s
Application throughput
0.7–2.1 Mbit/s
0.27 Mbit/s
Active slaves
7
Not defined; implementation dependent
Security
56/128-bit and application layer user defined
128-bit
AES
with
Counter Mode CBC-MAC
and application layer user defined
Robustness
Adaptive fast frequency hopping,
FEC
, fast
ACK
Adaptive frequency hopping, Lazy Acknowledgement, 24-bit CRC, 32-bit Message Integrity Check
Latency (from a non-connected state)
Typically 100 ms
6 ms
Total time to send data (det.battery life)
100 ms
3 ms
Voice capable
Yes
No
Network topology
Scatternet
Scatternet
Power consumption
1 W as the reference
0.01 to 0.5 W (depending on use case)
Peak current consumption
<30 mA
<15 mA
Service discovery
Yes
Yes
Profile concept
Yes
Yes

Authentication
Verifies the identification of the devices that are communicating in the channel.
Confidentiality
Protecting the data from the attacker by allowing only authorized users to access the data.
Authorization
Only authorized users have control over the resources.
Bluetooth Security

Security Mode 1
Non-Secure Mode
Security Mode 2
Service level enforced security mode
Security Mode 3
Link-level enforced security mode
Security
Modes
of Bluetooth

Only security at this level is by the nature of the connection: data-hopping and short-distance
Bluetooth devices transmit over the unlicensed 2.45GHz radio band, the same band used by microwave ovens and cordless phones.(FHSS)
All Bluetooth devices employ “data-hopping”, which entails skipping around the radio band up to 1600 times per second, at 1MHz intervals (79 different frequencies)
Most connections are less than 10 meters, so there is a limit as to eavesdropping possibilities
Mode 1: No Security

Service Access depends on device:
Trusted devices have unrestricted access to all services, fixed relationship to other devices
Untrusted devices generally have no permanent relationship and services that it has access to are limited.
Unfortunately, all services on a device are given the same security policy, other than application layer add-ons.
Mode 2: Service Level Security

Services can have one of 3 security levels:
Level 3
: Requires Authentication and Authorization.  PIN number must be entered.
Level 2
: Authentication only, fixed PIN ok.
Level 1
: Open to all devices, the default level. Security for legacy applications, for example.
Mode 2: Service Security Levels

Security is implemented by symmetric keys in a challenge-response system.
Security implementations in Bluetooth units are all the same, and are publicly available:
http://www.bluetooth.com/pdf/Bluetooth_11_Specifications_Book.pdf
Critical ingredients
PIN, BD_ADDR, RAND(), Link and Encryption Keys
Mode 3: Link level security

0x2
蓝牙的信任模式
受信任：设备已与另一设备建立固定关系，且对所有服务的访问不受限制。
不受信任：虽然已成功通过身份验证，但设备只能访问一组受限制的服务。
0x3
设备的可发现性
BD_ADDR
0x4
蓝牙安全服务
基于挑战
/
应答
(
Challenge/Response)
方式执行身份验证。
PIN
码
/
SSP
0x5
其他安全功能
自适应跳频
E0
加密算法
不可见性
配对
蓝牙安全运作流程

Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security
Security Issue
Remarks
Strength of the Random Number Generator (RNG) is unknown.
RNG may produce periodic numbers that reduces the strength of authentication mechanism.
Short PINs are allowed.
Such weak PINs are used to generate link and encryption keys that are easily predictable.
Encryption key length is negotiable.
More robust initialization key generation procedure should be developed.
No user authentication exists.
As only device authentication is provided, application security and user authentication can be employed.
Stream cipher is weak and key length is negotiable.
Robust encryption procedure and minimum key length should be decided and passed as an agreement.

Problems with the standard Bluetooth Security (Cont’d)
Security Issue
Remarks
Privacy can be compromised if the BD_ADDR is captured and associated with a particular user.
Once the BD_ADDR is associated with a particular user, that user’s activity can be logged. So, loss of privacy can be compromised.
Device authentication is simple shared key challenge response.
One-way authentication may be subjected to man-in-middle attacks. Mutual authentication is a good idea to provide verification.

Denial of service
Makes the device unusable and drains the mobile device battery.
Fuzzing attacks
Sending malformed messages to the
bluetooth
device.
Blue jacking
Uses IMEI identifier to route all the incoming calls.
Blue
snarfing
Causes harm when the user sends the data to the other user.
Security Threats

Scanning

Bluetooth Low Energy

BLE Primer
Standby:
Low Power Mode. Receiver and transmitter switched ‘off’
Advertising:
Used by low power ‘Server’. Only transmitter required.
Scanning:
Used by ‘Client’. Receiver listens to advertising channels.
Initiating:
‘Server’ sends connection request
Connection:
After scanning, ‘Client’ responds to ‘Server’ advertisement
